title: Chickpea Cookie Dough
tagline: Edible cookie dough with 6g of fiber hiding in it.
description: The fibermaxxing trend's flagship snack: chickpeas, peanut butter, and maple blitzed into an edible cookie dough that's safe raw (no flour, no egg), packs real fiber and protein, and tastes far better than it has any right to.
ingredients: 1 can (425g/15 oz) chickpeas, drained, rinsed hard, and patted dry
2 Tbsp oat flour (or blitz oats) - only if needed to thicken
steps: Rinse the chickpeas until the water runs clear, then rub them dry in a clean towel - this also sloughs off some skins. The drier the chickpeas, the less bean the dough tastes.
Food-process chickpeas, peanut butter, maple syrup, vanilla, and salt for a full 2-3 minutes, scraping down twice, until completely smooth. Under-processing is why people think this recipe is bad.
Too loose? Add oat flour a tablespoon at a time until it scoops like dough.
Pulse in the chocolate chips (or stir by hand to keep them whole).
Chill 30 minutes - cold is where it stops tasting healthy.
Eat with a spoon, stuff into dates, or roll into balls and keep in the fridge up to 5 days.
notes: The math: a can of chickpeas carries roughly 20g fiber and 20g protein across the batch - about 6g fiber per serving with the peanut butter.
This is dough, not dough-for-baking - no leavening, no egg; it stays dough.
Natural peanut butter (just peanuts and salt) keeps it scoopable; the sweetened kind makes it stiff and cloying.
testedNote: Three full minutes in the processor and a hard chill are what separate 'surprisingly great' from 'hummus with chips.'

verdict.note: Nobody guesses the chickpeas. Nobody. The fibermaxxing girlies are right about this one.

troubleshooting: Tastes like hummus → under-processed or wet chickpeas; blitz longer, dry harder.
Grainy → cheap blender or big-flake skins; peel more skins and process in a food processor, not a bullet.
Too stiff → dry natural PB from the bottom of the jar; loosen with a teaspoon of milk.
Too sweet → sweetened peanut butter stacking on the maple; use natural and re-taste before adding syrup.
